article.title: Adding Benchmaxxer Repellant to the Open ASR Leaderboard

harvestedTerms.summary: Hugging Face's Open ASR Leaderboard team announces the addition of private, high-quality English speech datasets from Appen and DataoceanAI to combat 'benchmaxxing' — the practice of optimizing models specifically for leaderboard metrics without genuine real-world improvement. The article introduces anti-contamination strategies for ASR benchmarking, including hidden test sets and macroaveraged metrics across accents and speech styles.

article.title: OpenAI Campus Network: Student club interest form

harvestedTerms.summary: OpenAI's Campus Network announcement is a sign-up form for student clubs to partner with OpenAI for AI learning events, workshops, and early tool access. The article is almost entirely form fields and navigation boilerplate with no substantive AI concepts, terminology, or analysis.

Ben Thompson's Stratechery piece analyses the hardware implications of the inference shift — the transition from GPU-dominated AI compute (optimised for training) to a heterogeneous mix including specialised inference chips like Cerebras' wafer-scale engine. The article covers GPU architecture, KV cache memory dynamics, prefill vs. decode distinctions, and why inference workloads have fundamentally different hardware requirements than training. Cerebras' IPO is the news hook, but the analytical substance is a detailed breakdown of why the inference era may require purpose-built silicon distinct from Nvidia's GPU dominance.

mustReadFlagged.reason: A clear-eyed, technically grounded breakdown of why inference and training impose fundamentally different hardware requirements — and what that means for the future of the AI chip market. Thompson's hardware literacy here is unusually good for a strategy writer; the prefill–decode–KV cache analysis is worth reading for anyone trying to understand why 'more GPUs' may not be the whole answer to AI scaling.

article.title: Your AI Use Is Breaking My Brain

harvestedTerms.summary: Simon Willison links approvingly to Jason Koebler's essay coining 'Zombie Internet' — a more nuanced alternative to 'Dead Internet Theory' describing the messy hybrid ecology of humans, bots, and AI-assisted actors intermingling online. The piece introduces a useful framing term for AI-saturated, authenticity-degraded online spaces.

mustReadFlagged.reason: Koebler's 'Zombie Internet' framing is the kind of terminological intervention worth paying attention to — it sharpens a real and growing problem (AI-saturated, authenticity-ambiguous online spaces) with more precision than 'bots' or 'AI slop' alone offer. Worth reading for the coinage and the argument behind it.

article.title: Why Leaders Should Let Minor Mistakes Slide

harvestedTerms.summary: An HBR article about management strategy — specifically whether leaders should overlook minor employee mistakes. The article excerpt provides almost no substantive content beyond metadata, navigation, and author/publication information. No AI terminology is present.

article.title: Google stopped a zero-day hack that it says was developed with AI

harvestedTerms.summary: Google's Threat Intelligence Group reports the first confirmed AI-assisted zero-day exploit in the wild, identifying hallucinated CVSS scores and LLM-style formatting as forensic signals in attacker-written Python code. The article also notes hackers using persona-driven jailbreaking and targeting AI system components like connectors and autonomous skills.

definitionReviews.reason: No source material addresses parameter counts, MoE architectures, or related concepts. Definition remains accurate and current.
The Google zero-day article touches on AI-assisted exploitation but provides no substantive detail in the excerpt to justify revising the definition. The core definition remains sound.
No source material addresses agentic permission behavior. Definition stands.
